data.title: Pzena Global Value ADR Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Global equities began the quarter on solid footing but subsequently reversed course amid concerns surrounding the software sector, driven in part by new AI developments from Anthropic (ANTHRO). These concerns, coupled with an escalating conflict in the Middle East, contributed to broader unease about the global economy and the private credit market. The United States was our portfolio's weakest-performing region, declining mid-single digits. We outperformed the MSCI World Index, driven by our investments in the materials sector, where stock selection and an overweight position benefited performance. Our detracting sectors were financials, health care, and industrials.

The largest contributions came from the materials and energy sectors, as oil prices broke above $100 per barrel. Dow, Inc. (DOW) was the largest individual contributor, as approximately 65% of its ethane crackers utilize low-cost natural gas feedstock, positioning the company to benefit from higher oil prices. Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) was the second-largest contributor, as consensus estimates for 2026 and 2027 continued to rise, driven by a more than 100% increase in spot memory prices. Nokia (NOK) was higher on investor optimism surrounding its AI-RAN prospects, while its network infrastructure unit continued to exhibit solid growth.

The portfolio's largest detractors were in the financials and health care sectors. Cognizant (CTSH) reported strong bookings and market share gains, but investor focus shifted to the sharing of AI-driven productivity gains with clients, which weighed on the stock. Capital One detracted as management guided to higher expenses related to the rollout of the Discover Financial Services network, while broader concerns around private credit risks and a potential economic slowdown added pressure. Humana (HUM) detracted as the company disclosed an unexpected 25% increase in Medicare Advantage plan membership, eliciting concerns of adverse selection, though growth in its 4-star plan membership should help offset some of the added medical costs.

We added to our positions in Accenture (ACN), Publicis (PUBGY), and Daikin (DKILY), all on weakness. Publicis, like Accenture, has been caught up in the AI-disruption selloff that characterized this quarter. Daikin, a global leader in air conditioning systems, is facing macroeconomic uncertainty across several geographies and tariff pressures, and we believe there is potential for margin improvement and a renewed focus on capital efficiency. We initiated a position in PT Bank Rakyat Indonesia (BKRKY), an Indonesian commercial bank trading at a depressed valuation due to weak macroeconomic conditions and elevated losses in its microfinance segment. We view it as a high-quality franchise well positioned to participate in Indonesia's long-term growth despite near-term challenges. To fund these investments, we exited our position in Medtronic (MDT) on valuation and trimmed our positions in Barclays (BCS), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (TSMC) (TSM), ArcelorMittal (MT), and Dow on appreciation.

Recent performance has been challenged by a combination of macroeconomic concerns, uncertainty around the impact of technological change across industries, and idiosyncratic developments weighing on the short-term fundamentals of a few large, high-conviction holdings. Despite these headwinds, we believe the current environment provides an exceptional long-term opportunity, given the portfolio's starting valuation and the breadth, magnitude, and quality of the opportunity set.

data.title: Alphabet Q1: First Signs Of Depreciation Pressuring EPS Are Here
data.description:
I am downgrading my rating on Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) heading into the Q1 2026 earnings release on April 29th, as I'm concerned that the AI CapEx cycle is moving into the income statement.

Yes, I'm talking about depreciation, as those GPUs and TPUs are coming online. That has a double-edged sword.

On one hand, it’s a tailwind for the cloud segment, and I want to believe it may be one for Search as well. In fact, the latter is one of the factors that I will closely monitor in the Q1 print.

On the other hand, the Street is projecting a mid-single-digit decline in EPS in Q1. In other words, as hinted by CEO Sundar Pichai in Q4, depreciation is ticking up, and that's not good news for earnings, particularly when the company has been slow to lay off employees in comparison to Meta (META) or Microsoft (MSFT).

As I discuss in this piece, I don't think there is an asymmetric upside in buying prior to the Q1 print. Therefore, I downgrade to a Hold.

Three Factors I'm Monitoring In Q1 2026

First, I want to see progress on the AI front. Specifically, AI Search.

As seen above, the growth rate of Google Search (and other) revenue accelerated since Q1 2025. It's impossible to know whether that was driven by AI (think: Gemini-powered ad matching) or simply by stronger consumer spending.

Based on the chart above, which shows the change in real PCE in the U.S. over the last few years, I don’t think this factor drove Alphabet's growth rate in the past 12 months.

From what I'm reading in the last earnings call, back in Q4 2025, search usage hit a record, AI Mode queries per user in the US doubled since launch, and AI Mode queries were 3x longer than traditional searches.

As you may know, Google is already testing ads within AI Mode responses. Unfortunately, management hasn't disclosed any revenue figures yet from this new ad surface. If they do in Q1 (unlikely, as AI Mode ads are still in testing mode), I would monitor that figure going forward.

Second, I would closely monitor the cloud segment, which showed a spike in growth last quarter.

Google Cloud exited Q4 at a revenue run rate above $70B, with backlog up 55% sequentially and more than double on a YOY basis.

Notably, Cloud AI demand was broad, with nearly 75% of Google Cloud customers using Google’s vertically optimized AI, according to CEO Sundar Pichai during the last earnings call.

In fact, AI customers used 1.8x as many products as non-AI customers, which shows how important this cohort is for the top line of this segment.

Right after Q4, KeyBanc raised its Alphabet target to $380 and estimated 2026 Google Cloud revenue at $91.8B. For reference, the consensus back then was $85.3B.

Speaking of the consensus, right after Q4, the Street's revenue forecast for the next 4 quarters has been upgraded for the third consecutive time.

Needless to say, I want to see a similar upgrade this week. That may only happen if results come in above consensus.

For reference, I included above the Street expectations for revenue and below the Street's models for EPS.

I discuss the third factor in the next section.

Risks

The third factor that I will closely monitor is CapEx. Here, I don't want to see more upgrades, as the $175B-$185B of FY 2026 CapEx is already ridiculously high. For reference, the company spent only $91.4 billion in capital expenditures in 2025. At the midpoint of the FY26 guidance, we're talking about a 97% YOY increase.

To be clear, I am not bothered by the pressure on free cash flows or share buybacks. My main concern is EPS, which will likely face pressure from depreciation as data centers come online.

For reference, depreciation increased last year, and based on the Q4 earnings call, management expects it to “meaningfully increase for the full year.”

In 2025, depreciation increased by nearly $6 billion, or 38%, from $15.3 billion in 2024, to $21.1 billion in 2025. Given the increase in our CapEx investments in recent years, we expect the growth rate in 2026 depreciation to accelerate in Q1, and meaningfully increase for the full year.

Given the growth trend in D&A seen above, I wouldn't be surprised to see Google join the likes of Meta or Microsoft in the layoff wave this year. So far, Alphabet's layoff announcements this year are minimal.

Valuation

I am not particularly bothered about the 29x (call it 30x) forward P/E multiple.

Compared to the rest of the hyperscalers, Alphabet is the second most expensive on the valuation front, trailing Amazon.

Given that Amazon’s top-line growth is weaker than Google's and AWS growth still trails Google Cloud, I don’t see why Google can’t rerate to the mid-30s (forward P/E).

Looking Ahead

You probably remember what happens with a hyperscaler when they show proof that AI is lifting the top-line growth. I'm talking about Meta's 10% jump post Q4 earnings, a topic I covered in this piece.

Unfortunately, Alphabet doesn't guide revenue for the next quarter or even for the entire fiscal year. Therefore, all we have are management's comments on the AI strategy in Search and Cloud and the results from the last quarter.

As a "fun fact," the company has reported revenue above consensus since Q1 2025. I don't expect to see a diversion from this trend in Q1 2026.

That said, I am a bit concerned about the chart below.

Call me crazy, but that looks pretty much like a double top to me. I think the biggest risk in Q1 is a CapEx upgrade. If this happens while the Q1 beat is soft, I think the stock may sell off post-earnings.

Will that break the bull case?

In my view, it largely depends on whether the Cloud segment comes in below the Street's expectations. For reference, for the Q1 2026 print, the current Cloud revenue consensus appears to be about $18.4B, implying roughly 50% y/y growth.

Overall, I prefer to sit on my hands before the Q1 earnings release, as I want to see progress before I build my conviction on this stock again.

data.title: Rollins: Despite Margin Declines, I Remain Confident In This Niche Market Leader
data.description:
Earlier this year, I stated Rollins, Inc. (ROL) was a “Hold” as even though I believed Rollins was (and continues to be) a high-quality business, its valuation was a little too stretched for my liking.

So far in 2026, Rollins is down more than 5% YTD while the S&P 500 is up over 4%

For long-term investors seeking an exceptional business that can survive in the age of artificial intelligence, I believe Rollins fits the bill. Let’s dig into the company’s most recent quarter as I discuss why I’m building an initial position here.

Company Updates

Rollins made three acquisitions in the quarter for a total of $18 million. However, the company’s largest acquisition year-to-date occurred after the first quarter ended as Rollins purchased Romex Pest Control, LLC (Romex) for $90 million in early April. Romex is a pest control company with numerous locations in Texas, Oklahoma, Louisiana, and Mississippi.

Here’s what Rollins’ CEO Jerry Gahlhoff had to say about this acquisition on the company’s last earnings call:

Earlier this month, we announced our acquisition of Romex Pest Control, a top 40 pest management company according to PCT top 100 rankings. Romex provides us with entry points into new markets while enabling them to further scale their operations and expand service offerings to their existing customer base. Most importantly, they have a strong people and customer-focused culture, and we were thrilled to welcome our new Romex teammates to the Rollins family.

To me, this seems like another outstanding acquisition that gives Rollins further access to the southern part of the United States.

Next, I’d like to touch on another factor that I mentioned in my original piece about Rollins, and that’s the company’s ongoing investments in personnel and training. I think it’s an aspect of many businesses that often isn’t emphasized enough, but I believe it can play a major role in the quality of service a customer receives. I thought this was an interesting quote from Gahlhoff as he talked about how the business has become more complex over the years:

…When I started in this business decades ago, this business was a lot simpler. And I did pest control and I did termite work. And the options that we had -- I had to learn about and what I had to do were relatively simple 30 years ago. Today, you've heard Ken talk about having 9 shots on goal. And when you have to train people to be able to be experts and knowledgeable, both on the service and the sales side for the complexity of all the things that our team does, that takes time. It takes experience. It's harder and harder. You can't just get somebody up and running in a few weeks like it was 30 years ago when I started in this business.

High-quality training, I believe, translates into more knowledgeable and more confident employees, which will result in great service, which will increase the odds a customer would come back to Rollins again.

I also think there's an aspect of knowledge transfer that because Rollins has so many companies under their umbrella, they can share experiences and knowledge, making employees in the field better able to deal with numerous scenarios. This further enhances the company’s moat and reaffirms Rollins’ status as a top company in this niche market.

Financials

In Q1 2026, Rollins generated revenue of roughly $906 million, which is an increase of more than 10% compared to Q1 2025. Of this 10%, 6.6% was from organic growth, and the rest came from acquisitions.

As you can see below, compared to Q1 2025, operating income and net income grew just like total sales:

For the quarter, gross margins did decline, coming in at 50.8% compared to 51.4% in the prior year’s first quarter. Management noted this was due to higher insurance and claims as well as some payroll costs as the business is preparing for its peak season.

Management noted they expect margins to improve as business ramps up over the next few quarters. The management team did state they continue to believe their targets of 7% to 8% of organic revenue growth and 2% to 3% revenue growth from M&A will be achievable. Additionally, the team expects cash flow will continue to convert at a rate exceeding 100% for the rest of the current fiscal year.

Rollins shareholders can appreciate that weighted average shares outstanding have declined over the last twelve months and the company’s amount of dividends paid per share has increased. In the current quarter, Rollins paid dividends to shareholders totaling $88 million.

Rollins has a decent Balance Sheet as you can see below:

Rollins doesn’t have enough current assets to cover all of the company’s current liabilities, although a large portion of the company's current liabilities balance is unearned revenue.

As I noted in a prior article, I don’t love that the company is goodwill heavy because it could lead to impairment charges, yet I understand in Rollins' case goodwill will likely be larger given the amount of M&A activity occurring. Rollins is a solid cash-generating company, so overall I'm not too worried about the company's current liabilities or long-term debt, as the business has a good debt-to-equity balance with retained earnings continuing to grow.

Risks

Rollins has numerous risks, many of which are mentioned in the risk factors section of the company’s most current 10-K filing. I’m going to touch on a few that I believe are the most noteworthy.

First, Rollins is at risk of weather and climate changes and seasonality that impact the company’s bottom line. As noted this quarter, January was a particularly bad month for the company as they only saw organic growth of 6.6%, which is below the company’s estimates. Yet, this is the yearly lull for the business, so that’s to be expected. In March, growth greatly improved as the business had total growth of 12% and organic growth over 8%. I think most likely this trend will continue, and the business will see growth continue around this level, with January being an outlier.

As M&A is central to Rollins’ investment thesis, acquisition integration and company selection will be a risk for this business. Now, as Rollins is a very established and historic company, they have a history of making appropriate acquisitions, and they have a disciplined and strategic approach to their M&A activity. The business does have a sizable amount of goodwill on their balance sheet, as I noted, so a failure to successfully integrate an acquisition could lead to goodwill impairments hitting the P&L and negatively impacting earnings.

Valuation

Rollins has a valuation grade of an "F" at Seeking Alpha:

As Rollins is a profitable and established business, I believe price to earnings is the best valuation metric. On both a trailing and forward P/E GAAP basis, Rollins currently far exceeds the sector median. It’s not entirely shocking that Rollins has a much higher P/E compared to the sector median, as it’s a high-quality business, and thus many investors are willing to pay up.

On a historic basis, the current forward and trailing P/E GAAP for Rollins are lower than the company’s five-year averages, and if you look at the below, you see the company's forward P/E ratio (non-GAAP) has dipped below the 48-52 range that the business had been in for most of last year:

From another viewpoint, in using a reverse discounted cash flow model with a discounted rate of 10% and a terminal rate of 3%, Rollins would need assumed growth of over 18% to justify the current share price:

Analysts believe Rollins can grow in the high single digits over the next few years. I think that’s a reasonable assumption, yet I believe the business could get into double-digit growth with successful M&A acquisitions.

Conclusion

Rollins is a high-quality business that I believe long-term investors can buy and hold and sleep easy at night knowing this historic company isn’t going anywhere. With AI continuing to disrupt much of the tech industry, I don’t believe that’s something you can say about many businesses these days.

That being said, Rollins is expensive. However, it’s been expensive for a long time, as the company’s historic five-year averages illustrate. Thus, I believe investors should feel comfortable buying or at least establishing a position at these levels. While I've initiated a small position, I plan to add should the stock price fall back to or below $50.

data.title: Tesla Q1 Earnings: Catalysts Galore (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Tesla FQ1 Earnings Report

My last analysis on Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) stock was published on Mar 10 under the title of “Tesla: Poised To Start A New Wright's Law Curve.” The analysis focused on the updates of its Optimus Gen 3 robotics at that time, explored the potential of the company to start a new Wright’s Law-driven cost decline, and rated the stock as a buy. Since then, the company has issued its earnings report (ER) for FQ1 2026. Overall, I consider Tesla’s FQ1 2026 ER as a pivotal point marking its transition from a pure-play electric vehicle manufacturer to a diversified artificial intelligence company, a consideration that has prompted me to upgrade the rating to Strong Buy. Out of the many updates provided in the ER, this follow-up article will concentrate on 3 areas to stay on point: its margins, the growth of service revenues, and also the developments of its FSD (full self-driving).

As a brief recap of the ER, TSLA reported a normalized EPS of $0.41 on total revenue of $22.3 billion, as shown in the next chart. Both figures beat the consensus estimates of the Street. More importantly, both gross margins (21.2% on a GAAP basis) and operation margins (4.2%) showed a substantial YOY expansion of 478 and 214 bp (basis points), respectively, as seen. Next, I will detail the implications of these expansions and argue why I expect further profitability boosts ahead.

TSLA Stock: Growing Service Revenue Met Declining Carbon Credits

Out of the many factors that fueled the profit growth and margin expansion, the role of service revenues caught the most of my attention. As seen in the chart above, services and other revenues surged by 42% and hit $3.745 billion in the past quarter. These sales thus have grown to be sizable proportions, representing more than 23% of its automotive revenue (of $16.2 billion) and surpassing the sales from its energy generation and storage segment. This growth suggests to me that the company’s future is becoming increasingly decoupled from simple hardware volume.

The decoupling is further evidenced by—and/or is of particular importance because of—the role of the carbon credits in its profit streams. To wit, according to the following report, Tesla’s carbon credit totaled $1.993 billion in 2025, a large drop (of 28%) from its 2024 peak level of $2.763 billion. More observations from the data are quoted from the report below. I fully share the insight that Tesla’s further growth and/or margin will depend less and less on the volume growth of these credits (and the highlights in the quotes were added by me).

Thus, by prioritizing high-margin services over vehicle price cuts and/or regulatory credits (which are ultimately unpredictable), I see a brighter prospect of substantial EPS growth with enhanced margins and lower volatility (emphasis added):

The EV maker has confirmed its role in carbon credit pooling. This means it shares emissions credits with other automakers. This helps them meet regional rules, especially in Europe. Tesla sells extra zero-emission credits to partner automakers under pooling agreements. In return, they receive payments. The 2025 data shows that carbon credits are still high-margin and important. However, they no longer provide steady support each quarter. Their effect on operating margin now relies on timing, regulatory cycles, and year-end compliance needs, not steady growth.

TSLA FSD And Robotaxi Updates

As aforementioned, my overall impression from the ER is the transition of TSLA from an EV maker to a diversified artificial intelligence. My last article focused on the progress of its Optimus robotics. Here, I want to elaborate more on the progress of its Full Self-Driving (FSD) and also the monetization potential.

In Q1, TSLA continued seeing rapid growth of its FSD adoption. As shown in the next chart, since the introduction of its V12 and beyond platform, the total miles driven with FSD grew from about 1 billion miles in the March quarter of 2024 to more than 9 billion miles in the past quarter with an accelerating curve. In terms of subscriptions, TSLA reported record net new FSD additions of 180K in Q1, with total active subscriptions now reaching 1.28 million. To echo my above point, I expect such growth to trigger nonlinear EPS growth, as the marginal cost of FSD is near zero in my assessment. Unlike traditional manufacturing (of EV and/or energy storage units), where growth is linear and capital-intensive, the FSD software suite should operate on a near-zero marginal cost per additional user once the R&D threshold is cleared.

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

Another catalyst closely related to the FSD development is TSLA’s robotaxi. Per the Q1 ER, the company has deployed robotaxis in a total of 4 cities (SF Bay Area, Austin, Dallas, and Houston), with 3 of them operating in an unsupervised mode. The company is preparing to expand robotaxis to 5 additional markets currently. However, the expansion seems to be progressing slower than the timeline the company had announced earlier. In Q1, TSLA described these expansions as “preparations underway,” as seen in the ER slide below, a change from the 1H26 target announced earlier. I can see reasons why it’s a good idea to temper the pace of new rollouts until the safety of its v15 FSD software is thoroughly tested. But I did not hear any specific timeline provided for the v15 release in the ER.

In terms of valuation, the stock is pricey, especially in bottom-line-oriented ratios. As seen in the next chart, TSLA’s valuation ratios are quite expensive both in absolute and also relative terms. In absolute terms, its FY1 P/E ratio is now about 179x, a quite extreme reading. In relative terms, the other Mag 7 stocks are priced with P/E ratios “only” around 27x on average. However, for a high-growth company, I suggest investors look past the accounting P/E ratios and consider other metrics such as P/E based on owner’s earnings and growth-adjusted valuation ratios. These aspects were the focus of my other articles, and the valuation premium is far lower than what the headline P/E ratio suggests in my assessment. This link provides an example of my assessment of its owners’ earning P/E ratio. And as another reflection, its EV/sales and P/sales ratios are far better aligned with the other Mag 7 peers, as shown in the chart below.

To recap, this TSLA revisit was triggered by the updates in its Q1 ER. A few updates left an especially strong impression on me and signaled the transition of TSLA from a capital-intensive EV manufacturer to a high-margin physical AI powerhouse. I expect this transition to further deepen in the near future, to further expand its profit margin, and also to help shield its profit growth from unpredictable extrinsic factors. These considerations have led me to see a further skewness in its return/risk curve than at the time of my last writing and prompted me to upgrade my rating to Strong Buy.

data.title: BNY Mellon Global Fixed Income Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The first quarter began with the belief that the global economy might be about to see some acceleration in growth coincide with a continued, albeit slow moderation in inflation, allowing further rate cuts in the US and UK. However, the combined US/Israeli attack on Iran and the expansion into a continuing conflict abruptly altered that perspective. The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, which has significantly reduced the global flow and supply of oil from the Middle East, drove oil and gas prices higher, and has meant a significant reassessment of the growth and inflation outlook ahead. Markets have reversed course on interest rate expectations, with some anticipation that central banks may need to raise rates in the near term in an attempt to stifle inflation before it becomes entrenched. Debate as to how long the war will last and how long oil prices will remain high has become a focal topic, with a lack of clarity as to what the main objectives of the key protagonists in the conflict are. Meanwhile, the Russia/Ukraine war has continued, passing the four-year mark, with little sign of progress towards a resolution.

“We believe the Middle East conflict is likely to have a combined dampening effect on global growth largely through its effect on energy prices and a range of other commodities, while also putting upward pressure on inflation.”

Performance Summary

For the quarter ended March 31, 2026, the Fund’s Class I shares returned –0.79%, excluding sales charges. In comparison, the Fund’s unmanaged benchmark, the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index (Hedged), returned –0.15% for the same period.

Developed market government bonds rose across the quarter, driven sharply higher in March as the war with Iran led energy prices upwards, which in turn reversed expectations for declining inflation into concerns that a higher spike may be protracted. In the US, 10-year US Treasury yields rose by 15 basis points (BP) to 4.32%. German 10-year government bond yields also rose by 15bp to reach 3.00%, Italian 10-year yields rose 36bp, while in Japan and Australia they increased by 29bp and 23bp respectively. UK gilt yields increased even more significantly, rising 44bp over the quarter to 4.92%, rising substantially in March, more than reversing the modest decline experienced in February. Yield levels in the emerging markets also increased, with the J.P. Morgan Emerging Market Bond Index ending 50bp higher at 6.37%.

Credit markets were also weaker, with spread levels widening, moving above the recent historically low levels, although not substantially so. With government yields rising too, yields on corporate bonds rose higher still. The option-adjusted spread (OAS) over governments for the Bloomberg US Corporate Index ended the quarter 11bp wider at 89bp, with the related Bloomberg US Credit Index widening by 10bp, to 83bp. Spreads increased more significantly in high yield markets, with the Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Index was 51bp wider at 317bp.

The Bloomberg US Investment Grade Corporate Index declined for the first quarter. Oil related sectors, such as independent energy, refining and oil field services companies did well, generating positive excess returns, but most other corporate sectors were negative. Supranationals and foreign local governments and agencies were also positive. Non-cyclical consumer products held up fairly well, as did construction machinery and cable satellite. However, media entertainment was notably negative, along with metals and mining, consumer services, technology and finance and insurance generally, but particularly life insurance.

Performance Review

The strategy underperformed its reference benchmark and had a negative total return in the first quarter. Duration positioning contributed positively, chiefly resulting from long exposure to 10-year US and German bunds. However, yield curve trades detracted significantly from performance. Our US “curve steepener” (a long position in 10-year year US Treasuries offset against a short position in 30-year Treasuries) was the largest negative contributor to performance. Both developed and emerging-market country selection, such as long positions in Korea, Australia, Colombia, and Brazil, weighed on alpha. Inflation positioning also detracted, notably from long 30-year US dollar inflation protection versus euro and British pound. Credit strategy and mortgage-backed security exposure further contributed to underperformance. Foreign currency positioning was a bright spot, however, due to positioning in Australian dollar, Swedish krona, Norwegian krone, Japanese yen, and Canadian dollar.

The strategy made several notable positioning adjustments this quarter. Duration remained broadly neutral, with a reduction in 10-year German bunds being offset by an increase in 10-year US Treasuries. Curve positioning was reduced, specifically in the 10s30s Japanese yen flattener and 10s30s US dollar steepener. Emerging-market local positions shifted modestly, trimming Brazil exposure while adding to Peru and Thailand, all of which were versus US Treasuries. 30-year British pound inflation protection was reduced by the same amount 30-year US dollar inflation protection was increased. Mortgage-backed securities beta reductions in 30-year pools early in the quarter were subsequently reversed. Credit beta increased, driven by higher exposure to US dollar and euro credit, with a tilt toward more BBB-rated and less A- and BB-quality names. Synthetic credit exposure was also increased. Foreign currency positioning saw increases in US dollar, British pound, Swedish krona, New Zealand dollar, and Swiss franc, balanced by reductions in Japanese yen, Norwegian krone, and euro.

Market Outlook

We believe the Middle East conflict is likely to have a combined dampening effect on global growth largely through its effect on energy prices and a range of other commodities, while also putting upward pressure on inflation. As the conflict continues, its impact on global economic growth is likely to become more severe and prolonged, potentially creating significant problems for many governments already facing difficult fiscal challenges. Central banks, which were either expected to cut rates further or at least remain on hold, may find themselves needing to increase official rates in the face of higher inflation levels and prospects. Further, other geopolitical crises may continue, notably the Russia/Ukraine war, as the West focuses on the Middle East. For the US, the ongoing conflict in the Middle East is heightening risks to the current baseline forecasts for both economic growth and inflation. We see GDP growth of 2.3% in 2026 and 2.2% in 2027, with inflation expected to be 2.6% this year, moderating to 2.4%. However, the potential for variance around those forecasts is elevated. While immediate inflationary pressures are likely to delay any potential rate cuts by the Fed, the effects on growth raise the possibility of further weakness in the labor market. Given the heightened risks to growth stemming from elevated US oil and gas prices, we now expect the US central bank to keep rates unchanged for the time being, and should economic growth deteriorate, rate cuts may likely follow later in the year or in 2027. We see the Fed funds rate at around 3.5% in the next 12 months. We expect 10-year and 30-year Treasury yields to be around 4.05% and 4.70% respectively, though volatility is possible, particularly as the midterm elections approach.

The eurozone economies face headwinds from higher energy prices. As in most regions, the risk is that inflation may rise rapidly in 2026. We expect inflation to be around 2.0% in 2026, moderating slightly in 2027, and we see downside risks to our GDP growth forecasts of 1.5% in 2026 and 1.7% in 2027. We believe the ECB is the least likely of the central banks to dismiss the energy price spike and will be alert for any signs of second-round effects. However, the ECB begins from a stronger position than most others, with inflation close to target and inflation expectations consistent with its target. We expect 10-year German government bond yields to remain close to current levels over the next 12 months, at around 3.00%. However, increased borrowing at longer maturities could push 30-year yields up to 3.5% as the curve steepens.

In emerging markets, we were anticipating Chinese GDP growth to decelerate to just 4.0% in 2026 and further to 3.8% in 2027, even prior to the advent of hostilities in the Middle East. In the event of a widespread global slowdown, China's export sector, often considered a bulwark of the country's momentum, could come under further strain, putting ever greater pressure on the domestic economy to make up any shortfall. We expect inflation to remain positive, at around 1.0% in 2026 and be slightly higher in 2027. The People's Bank of China is likely to make little change to interest rates, while bond yields are expected to move higher over time. In other emerging markets, high energy prices could have a similar dampening effect on activity and push inflation upwards, undermining the ability of many central banks to reduce rates further. At the same time, a reversal of the weakening US dollar trend could further reduce the scope for more central bank rate cuts. If the US economy holds up more strongly than other regions, those countries with greater export exposure to the US market may experience some relative benefits.

Risks

Bonds are subject to interest-rate, credit, liquidity, call and market risks, to varying degrees. Generally, all other factors being equal, bond prices are inversely related to interest-rate changes and rate increases can cause price declines. Investing in foreign denominated and/or domiciled securities involves special risks, including changes in currency exchange rates, political, economic, and social instability, limited company information, differing auditing and legal standards, and less market liquidity. These risks generally are greater with emerging market countries. High yield bonds involve increased credit and liquidity risk than higher-rated bonds and are considered speculative in terms of the issuer's ability to pay interest and repay principal on a timely basis. The use of derivatives involves risks different from, or possibly greater than, the risks associated with investing directly in the underlying assets. Derivatives can be highly volatile, illiquid, and difficult to value and there is the risk that changes in the value of a derivative held by the portfolio will not correlate with the underlying instruments or the portfolio's other investments.

The Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index (Hedged) is a flagship measure of global investment-grade debt from 24 local currency markets. This multi-currency benchmark includes treasury, government-related, corporate and securitized fixed-rate bonds from both developed and emerging market issuers. Currency exposure is hedged to the U.S. dollar. J.P. Morgan Market Bond indices are a family of benchmarks designed to track the performance of various bond markets, including those in the US, emerging markets, and global markets. The Bloomberg US Investment Grade Corporate Index is a benchmark for the investment-grade, fixed-rate, taxable US corporate bond market. It includes USD-denominated securities publicly issued by US and non-US industrial, utility, and financial issuers. The Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Bond Index measures the USD-denominated, high yield, fixed-rate corporate bond market. Securities are classified as high yield if the middle rating of Moody's, Fitch and S&P is Ba1/BB+/BB+ or below. The Bloomberg US Credit Index measures the investment grade, US dollar-denominated, fixed-rate, taxable corporate and government related bond markets. Investors cannot invest directly in any index.

Mortgage-Backed Security (MBS) is an investment similar to a bond that is made up of a bundle of home loans bought from the banks that issued them. Investors in MBS receive periodic payments similar to bond coupon payments. A yield curve is a line that plots yields, or interest rates, of bonds that have equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is the total monetary or market value of all the finished goods and services produced within a country's borders in a specific time period. Inflation is the rate of increase in the cost of living. Inflation is usually quoted as an annual percentage, comparing the average price this month with the same month a year earlier. The option-adjusted spread (OAS) is the measurement of the spread of a fixed-income security rate and the risk-free rate of return, which is then adjusted to take into account an embedded option. A basis point (BP) is 1/100th of 1% and is commonly used to indicate interest rates or changes in rates in bonds and other financial instruments.

data.title: Butler National: From Gambling To Strong Aerospace Buy (Upgrade)
data.description:
Butler National Corporation (BUKS) is probably the oddball in my coverage, combining aerospace businesses with a gambling business. For some reason, that is a combination that works for investors. The stock has gained 33% since my last report, surpassing my $3.56 price target. Since I upgraded the stock from hold to buy in August 2025, the stock price has even increased 156%. While the stock price has performed above expectations, I also believe we need to be mindful about the risks. In this report, I discuss the risks and opportunities, the most recent earnings, and an update to my price target for Butler National Corporation.

Butler National Corporation: Aerospace And Gambling

Butler National Corporation is not a huge company, with only 360 employees and a $261.1 million market capitalization. The business operates with exposure to aerospace and gambling organized in two segments, which are Aerospace Products and Professional Services.

The Aerospace Products segment operates through four subsidiaries providing aerial surveillance products, displays, sensors, and transponders, among others. Modifications include the ability to install doors, TCAS systems, ISR systems, extended fuel tanks, and radar systems, while special mission electronics capabilities include cabling, electronic control systems, test equipment, and gun control units for Apache and Blackhawk helicopters, as well as land- and sea-based vehicles.

Through the Professional Services segment, the company operates the Boot Hill Casino with revenues from casino gaming, sportsbook revenues, and non-gaming casino revenues.

The big opportunity for Butler is in the aerospace products segment supported by expanding defense budgets. The risk that Butler does face as a smaller defense business and niche provider is cost inflation. The bigger a business is, the more it can generate leverage and absorption power to tackle rising costs. Butler does not have that opportunity. So, the current increase in oil prices, energy prices, inflation, and raw material costs is a risk for Butler, with near-term cost risk exceeding near-term lift for defense equipment driven by the war in Iran.

We also note that gambling can take a downturn when the economy does. For the gambling business, Butler relies on the local farmers driving the economy. With fertilizer prices and energy prices going through the roof, we may see pressure on the gambling business.

Aerospace Shows Strong Growth While Gambling Stalls

Butler’s Q3 revenues showed 27% growth in revenues to $21.2 million. By segment, we note that revenues for professional services remained stable as casino gaming revenues declined 5%, partially offset by 20.7% growth in sports wagering revenues and casino non-gaming revenues. Aerospace product sales grew 50% to $11.361 million and now account for nearly two-thirds of the revenues, with aircraft modification and avionics revenues climbing 38% and 39%, while special mission electronics sales grew 95%. That growth is not completely unexpected. Butler is not “passively” capitalizing on defense budget growth but is actively expanding its capacity. The Newton facility in Kansas, for instance, increases capacity 5x, and Butler is focusing on new growth drivers such as a program for a bigger cargo door for the King Air turboprop, large aircraft system integrations, new radomes for business jets, and special mission electronics, including a next-generation minigun control unit.

Sales growth was driven by North America, where 65% growth was recorded, and Asia, where 33% growth was recorded, but the latter only represents 3% of sales. In Europe, sales declined by more than 50%, which likely is more a timing issue than geographical demand weakness.

Operating income rose from $4 million to $9.2 million, indicating margin expansion from 19% to 34%, fully driven by growth in the aerospace products business.

Butler National Corporation Upgraded To 'Strong Buy'

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Butler National Corporation, I previously assumed a growth rate of 5.5% annually. With the nine months results in, that assumption, in my view, clearly understates the growth trajectory that the company is on. As a result, I have adapted the growth baseline to 11.5% with 16.5% annual growth in EBITDA and 27% growth in free cash flow. That is quite a high growth rate, but it may still be conservative. We also note that the valuation multiple for BUKS is set at a third of the peer group, and even then we get to a 13% upside for FY27. Given that the company will enter FY27 on the first of May, looking ahead at FY28 makes sense, and that would bring the price target to $5.77, indicating 42% upside, and justifies an upgrade to "Strong Buy."

For Butler National Corporation, I expect EBITDA to substantially increase as throughput increases in combination with higher-margin work. That should drive up free cash flow substantially, with capital intensity reducing to around 15% of operating cash flow by 2030. The company is expected to enter a net cash position by 2027 after repaying most of its maturing debt, and that also means that there is substantial space for the company to either accelerate share repurchases or explore acquisitions.

Conclusion: Butler National Corporation Strong Growth Triggers 'Strong Buy' Upgrade

For Butler National Corporation, I am upgrading my expectations and rating to "Strong Buy." The company does face some risk on cost inflation and the spending at the casino business due to increased prices of fertilizers, chemicals, and oil, which can affect both parts of the business. However, the company is executing a strong pivot from mixed business to an aerospace growth company.

data.title: SunScout Holding Targets IPO Despite High Valuation And Risks
data.description:
SunScout Is Seeking An IPO For A Business Shift

SunScout Holding Limited (SNSC) has filed to raise $22 million in an IPO of its Class A ordinary shares, according to an F-1 registration statement.

The company develops solar-powered products for various applications.

SNSC wants to pursue the autonomous mower market but has numerous execution & growth risks, and its valuation expectations at IPO are excessive.

My outlook on the SNSC IPO is to sell (avoid it)

What Does SunScout Do?

New Zealand-based SunScout Holding has developed solar-powered robotic mowers and other products and solar services that it is commercializing in commercial and consumer markets.

Most of its revenue has come from its solar power development solutions and engineering products and services, but management says the future growth driver of the company is going to be from its SunScout Products offerings:

  • Solar-powered robotic mowers - approaching market-ready

  • Solar shelter - in development

  • Go-Easy Campervan and EV Utility Van - in development

A pie chart showing the company's revenue contribution for the second half of calendar 2025 is shown here:

SunScout has distribution potential for its autonomous mowing product line in Germany, Australia, and New Zealand.

The company's mower product line is divided into three types:

  • Eco - residential

  • Pro - light commercial

  • ProMax - large-scale institutional

The firm is led by Chairman and Chief Executive Officer Mr. Edwin Cywinski, who has extensive experience in the commercial solar industry. His son, Marc Cywinski, is Chief Operations Officer.

SunScout has received $110,234 in fair market value investment from AE Equity Limited, Solerin Equity Limited, and Mr. Jacob Pretorius, among others.

What is SunScout’s Primary Market?

SunScout is positioning its future growth path as reliant on its Products group, so it may be evaluated based on the robotic mower market, which was an estimated $9.3 billion in 2025, according to a 2026 Grand View Research market research report.

The market is forecast to reach nearly $22 billion by 2033, which would represent a CAGR of 11.4% from 2026 to 2033.

If achieved, that growth would be a reasonably robust rate of growth.

The North American market accounted for the highest share by revenue, with 38.2% in 2025.

The chart below shows the historical and projected future growth path of the robotic lawn mowers' market worldwide through 2033 by battery capacity:

Competitors in the autonomous mower market include:

  • Husqvarna Group (HUSQF) - large incumbent

  • Segway Navimow - premium residential

  • Mammotion - innovator, large residential lawns

  • WORX - incumbent

  • Greenworks - innovator

  • Ecovacs - challenger from the vacuum sector

  • Sunseeker - Value segment

  • Robomow - smaller incumbent

  • Ambrogio - premium, strong in Europe

  • FireFly Automatix - new entrant

The primary growth drivers of the autonomous, battery-powered mower market will be the desire to automate landscaping work, a trend toward electrification of outdoor equipment, and demand for lower-emission, lower-noise lawn maintenance.

The company's strengths include its three-tiered product line, residential and commercial markets, and a multi-channel go-to-market strategy.

Weaknesses for SunScout are its early stage of primary market commercialization, small sales infrastructure, and development stage for distribution relationships.

The firm’s opportunities are primarily from a growing autonomous mower market and expansion into Europe and the U.S.

Threats to its prospects are improving technologies from incumbents and new entrants, distribution delays, and product reliability and safety requirements.

SunScout’s Recent Financial Results

The company has produced most of its revenue from its other business lines, which are partially funding its efforts in the mower industry.

Topline revenue growth of 17.1% for the first half of fiscal 2026 vs. the prior year's period was due to growth in its legacy business segments, i.e., its Solar Power Development Solutions and Engineered Products & Services, rather than growth from its future focus of SunScout Products (Mowers, etc).

SunScout’s IPO Plan

SNSC plans to sell four million Class A ordinary shares at a proposed midpoint price of $5.50 per share, for gross proceeds of $22 million, not including underwriter allotments.

Class A shareholders will receive one vote per share, and each Class B share will be entitled to twenty (20) votes per share.

Shareholder sale lock-ups will be for the typical 180-day period after the effective date of the prospectus.

However, the company may sell additional shares after only three months from the effective date, an unusual term that is not often seen in IPOs.

This means management might be planning to issue a follow-on offering relatively soon after the IPO, potentially causing greater share supply to come to market in a short period of time.

Management intends to use the net proceeds from the IPO as described here:

We currently intend to use the net proceeds from this offering for the following purposes: establish a manufacturing plan, marketing and promotion campaigns, product development, inventory, repayment of loan, payment under the Brightway MPA and working capital.

(Source: SEC)

The firm’s online IPO roadshow presentation is not currently available.

SunScout said it is not aware of any legal claims or proceedings that would have a material adverse effect on its financial condition or business.

Underwriters of the IPO are Dominari Securities and Revere Securities.

Capital And Liquidity Dynamics

As of December 31, 2025, SunScout had $893,278 in cash and equivalents and total liabilities of $3.5 million, of which approximately $2.4 million was in debt.

Free cash flow for the trailing twelve months ended December 31, 2025, was $698,677 after capital expenditures of $134,522, a modest capex intensity.

The company's MBIE debt was meaningful because it has a fixed interest rate of 11.84%, although the IPO’s proceeds will presumably be used to pay it off.

SunScout has a current ratio of almost 3x, which is healthy.

SunScout Is Seeking Expansion Capital Ahead Of Transition

SunScout wants public investors to fund its transition away from lower-potential business services to manufacturing autonomous, electric-powered mowing equipment.

The firm is still at the stage of finalizing its production plans, so it faces numerous execution risks in the coming quarters.

Also, SunScout’s historically high margins from its legacy service offerings will likely be cut in half once it begins production of its mower lines.

The company’s growth strategy involves establishing a U.S. manufacturing footprint, growing international operating complexity for this small New Zealand-based company.

SNSC also wants to follow a distributor-led international expansion model, which shows significant reliance on third parties to produce sales growth.

Management also intends to expend resources on direct-to-consumer via online sales, which can be expensive and take some time to dial in for positive ROI.

The overall market for autonomous mowers is expected to grow at a moderately robust rate of growth in the coming years, so the firm has positive industry growth dynamics in its favor.

But its growth plans require many things to go right, in the right sequence, and the firm will be dependent on distributors.

Related party transactions have been substantial and include a $5 million acquisition of the owner’s Brightway entity, more fully detailed here.

As to valuation, I’ve compared SunScout to the Husqvarna Group (HUSQF) for major metrics shown in the table below:

data.title: Capital Clean Energy Carriers: Stable LNG Cash Flows With A Rerating Catalyst
data.description:
Having completed a transition from container shipping to LNG and gas, Capital Clean Energy Carriers Corp. (CCEC) is a vastly different company from what it was a few years ago. They now mostly have multi-year revenue contracts. That implies stable cash flows but limited upside due to high capital needs; no surprise that the market values Capital Clean Energy around 10x earnings.

However, their recent joint venture deal could change all that. Right now, this is a single-ship JV, but if they can scale that, it could become a new economic model that is less capital-intensive and therefore deserving of higher multiples.

So we get an unusual setup. Purely by current fundamentals, the stock looks fairly valued and should be rated a Hold. However, with this new structural shift that the market doesn’t appear to be pricing in, there’s a new dynamic here. Downside is supported by contracted revenues; however, upside comes from multiple expansions based on the new JV deal(s). Therefore, I rate Capital Clean Energy a cautious Buy—not for its yield, nor even for its growth, but for potential rerating due to this transition.

What Has Changed

Capital Clean Energy is now a gas-focused shipping platform, having sold 14 container vessels since late 2023 (only one to go). Currently they have 12 LNG carriers in the water and 9 on order, with total committed CapEx of roughly $2.4 billion. They also have their first LCO2/multi-gas carrier. So their pivot to LNG/gas is complete.

We can see this reflected in their revenue profile. They now have ~$2.9 billion of contracted LNG revenues with an average duration of 6.9 years and optionally another $1.4 billion and 4 more years. This helps understand the current valuation of 10x earnings, based on stable and visible earnings, but not a growth story. For that growth story, we have the new JV structure.

A recent JV transaction marks a very interesting new phase. Capital Clean Energy is selling the LNG carrier Amore Mio I (a 2023-built, 174,000 cbm vessel) into a joint venture where it has 51% control with a partner from the BGN Group. The vessel is being valued at $230M, plus the JV has already secured a 10-year charter with another 6 years optional. The potential value of the charter is ~$485.6 million in total revenue through 2043.

On the surface, this looks like a standard partial asset sale. In reality, it is doing several things at once:

This structure lets Capital Clean Energy monetize assets and recycle capital under an extended contract without materially reducing its economic exposure. And this could be a template.

If Capital Clean Energy can repeat this structure for more ships, they could continue to lower equity per asset and improve return on capital, basically multiplying the entire structural benefits just outlined. That would make them basically a capital-recycling platform for contracted LNG vessels—a much different business from a traditional, balance sheet-heavy shipping operator.

This isn’t something the market seems to be pricing Capital Clean Energy in for.

Earnings Profile

We can see this difference between current valuation and growth potential from the JV in the earnings.

Capital Clean Energy reported quarterly revenue of ~$98 million, down ~6% YoY, and net income was ~$28 million. Flat revenues, modest growth—that's the unremarkable picture seen on the surface, and the reason why the stock trades as it does.

However, looking closely, it becomes clear that depreciation and interest expense together come to ~$45.8 million, or ~47% of quarterly revenue. That means operating performance as seen through reported net income is heavily distorted by Capital Clean Energy’s financing structure. In other words, the business may be generating stable charter income, but equity earnings are held back by the capital-intensive nature of the business. The same thing is seen in cash flow. Despite generating $232.8 million in operating cash flow in FY2025, most of it doesn’t flow to equity because of the huge capex requirement.

The JV structure becomes relevant right here. It basically gives wings to a balance-sheet-heavy business. It could enable Capital Clean Energy to decouple growth from capital intensity and perhaps cause the market to re-rate the stock.

Dividend And Balance Sheet

The high capital requirement limits the amount of cash that actually reaches shareholders. Capital Clean Energy has been paying quarterly dividends for 75 quarters. Currently it pays $0.15, or $0.60 annually, at roughly 3% yield. The payout ratio is low at ~30% of earnings. This supports sustainability but also shows that most cash is still going to capex and not getting back to shareholders. Thus, at just 3%, the dividend is working only as a support and doesn’t form a core part of the investment thesis. It is nice to have but doesn’t compensate for the capital intensity and doesn’t drive total returns.

Moving on, the balance sheet is still the key limitation. Debt is $2.3–$2.4 billion against ~$1.1–1.2 billion in equity, so leverage is significant, and servicing that debt holds back earnings. This limits equity upside, so they need to be disciplined in their capital allocation, especially where they are working through that huge $2+ billion new build CapEx cycle. This is exactly why the market continues to be conservative with its multiples. Until capital intensity improves, valuation expansion isn’t going to come from fundamentals alone, but from other things—for example, through more JVs kicking in.

Valuation

Like I noted already, Capital Clean Energy is now valued at roughly ~10x forward earnings, with an EPS of around $2. This is consistent with how the business is assessed today: a stable, contract-backed, but capital-heavy shipper with limited growth potential. If you take that angle, the stock looks fairly valued. But there’s rerating potential if the JV thing takes off. I have modeled these scenarios below:

Scenario Analysis

The base case here is where we are today—visible earnings, capped upside—translating into mid-single-digit returns, which includes the 3% dividend.

The bear case is about 25% below today’s level. It requires a weakening LNG cycle or balance sheet pressure. That could justify lower multiples, especially given the capital-heavy business and high borrowing from external sources.

The bull case forms my thesis. Earnings growth is minimal, as you can see, but there’s a somewhat higher multiple if the JV model can scale. If that happens, Capital Clean Energy should be able to reduce equity per asset and improve returns on capital. This could lead to a rerating, because then it becomes a different business model altogether. Even a partial shift towards an asset-light model can support a rerating toward 12–13x earnings.

The takeaway here is that while Capital Clean Energy looks fairly valued as it is now, the JV development says the investment case is not so static. On the one side, the downside is blocked by long-term contracted revenue, and on the other, the upside now has a narrative: Capital Clean Energy can scale these capital-recycling JVs. Even if earnings take time to grow, given the contracted nature, the new model can change the return profile in how earnings are perceived and valued.

That asymmetry—limited fundamental downside but credible rerating potential—is what supports a cautious Buy.

Conclusion

Capital Clean Energy today is in an interesting position. Its transition to LNG is complete, and it now operates with visible contracted cash flows. This supports the downside and justifies the current valuation. However, those same characteristics (along with capital intensity) limit upside. This is why Capital Clean Energy remains very range-bound, as seen in its 52-week chart.

The new JV structure could change that equation if it can be scaled up. In future quarters, this is something we will be looking for. If it scales, the business becomes a higher-return, less capital-heavy structure.

Current earnings do not reflect this shift, so current market valuation doesn’t reflect this either. But since the downside is supported already, this growth strategy is a credible pathway for multiple expansions and presents an inherent asymmetry in the stock.

Therefore, I rate Capital Clean Energy a cautious Buy—not for the yield or near-term growth, but for a change in market perception if there is such a structural transition.

data.title: Shopify Preview: Expensive, But Not Overhyped Ahead Of Q1
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I think Shopify (SHOP) is an interesting story in software and e-commerce. I also think that Shopify’s price has declined with much of the rest of the internet/software space. However, I believe the market is being overly concerned about near-term volatility and less concerned about the underlying strength of Shopify’s growth.

My investment thesis is that Shopify will become increasingly important as one of the fundamental platforms for what I call “new” digital commerce, particularly as a result of the growth of “agentic commerce.” Orders generated by AI-powered searches have grown significantly since 2025, and as you’ll see later on in this article, Shopify has entered into an agreement with Google regarding the development of a “Universal Commerce Protocol,” which will make it even easier for customers to complete checkout and pay for their products via AI. For me, this is one of the most underappreciated aspects of the story.

Shopify has continued to post high revenue growth rates. Currently over 30%, combined with new cohorts of higher productivity merchants and increased penetration within the enterprise segment. With respect to margins, I am confident that investors remain too conservative in their expectations. Operating expenses already came in below guidance in Q4, and the potential for Shopify to implement artificial intelligence internally is likely to keep costs lower than they were prior to doing so.

While valuation is certainly elevated in absolute terms, Shopify is still trading below some of its historical valuation averages. Furthermore, as mentioned earlier, I believe that Shopify’s fundamentals are significantly improved compared to where they have historically been.

Therefore, despite being expensive, I remain bullish on Shopify. While the company is not inexpensive, I firmly believe that it deserves a premium multiple if the company can deliver strong Q1 results and provide constructive Q2 guidance (and demonstrate initial success with its proposed “AI Commerce Cycle”).

Previous Quarter: A Strong Finish, With One Clear Question Mark

Shopify had a great fourth quarter in 2025. Gross Merchandise Volume (GMV) grew 31% YOY (year-over-year), as did revenues. To me, what matters isn't how well Shopify exceeded its earnings estimates. What matters is Shopify is continuing to grow so rapidly.

In terms of the growth areas of Shopify's businesses, "Merchant Solutions" grew about 35% from last year, while "Subscription Solutions" were up approximately 17%. GMV totalled almost $123.8 billion. Revenues came in at approximately $3.7 billion. Non-GAAP Operating Income was $760M with a non-GAAP operating margin greater than 20%. This is an area where many investors continue to be underestimating the magnitude of Shopify's operational leverage. Shopify is transitioning from being strictly a growth stock into a growth stock with real operational leverage.

Guidance for Q1 '26 was also quite positive. Management guided for Q1 revenue growth in the low-30% range and gross profit growth in the high-20% range, with free cash flow margins expected to remain in the low-to-mid teens. Shopify also announced a $2 Billion Buyback Program. I see this announcement as important. It appears to me that Shopify has sufficient cash generation to begin returning capital to shareholders via buybacks while maintaining their aggressive investments in Product, Payments, Enterprise and AI.

I also liked seeing improvement in new merchant cohorts. New merchants added in both '24 and '25 are generating GMV and revenue at a faster rate than previous merchant cohorts. While this may not seem like a big deal, I find this to be an extremely positive sign because it implies that Shopify is not simply adding more customers but is adding better customers.

My largest concern going forward remains margin mix. Merchant Solutions gross margin has come under pressure due to an increasing amount of volume moving through payments, which carries significantly lower gross margins than many other legacy revenue streams. I consider this debate to be valid. The business is certainly becoming more payment-heavy, which will negatively impact reported gross margin. However, I would rather own a company that is taking a larger percentage of transaction flows, even if it comes with a lower gross margin, than own a company that defends a higher gross margin profile but has less strategic relevance.

Upcoming Quarter Preview: Q1 2026

Q1 2026 is going to be all about one simple issue: "Is Shopify still getting bigger, or are we entering into a normalizing phase?"

My expectation for Q1 is a good, solid quarter again, GMV growth in the low-to-mid 30% range, and revenue growth around the low 30%. I believe this would be consistent with what has been provided by Shopify regarding their own guidance and with the evidence that Shopify continues to gain share in the e-commerce space. My expectation of the company's performance is neither too high nor too low. However, I expect the performance to exceed expectations.

I'm looking at 4 areas for confirmation on whether Shopify delivered a good quarter.

  1. First, I'm looking at GMV. If Shopify reports GMV greater than approximately $100 billion for the quarter, it will be a positive indicator that the platform is continuing to compound at a rapid pace. Second, I'd like to know how much was generated through North American activity compared to international activity. How much came from smaller merchants (SMB) vs. larger merchants (enterprise)? Finally, I'm interested in seeing how much of the growth came from larger merchants, particularly globally known brands. To me, the importance of the larger merchant story comes from its knack to influence investor sentiment and ultimately influence the valuation multiple assigned to Shopify. A platform that only uses small merchants will have a very different valuation model compared to a platform being used by global brands.
  2. Shopify is also evolving in terms of becoming more embedded within the transaction. Therefore, the second area I'd like to highlight is payments. Shop Pay, GPV penetration, and Merchant Solutions take rate are all significant indicators because they show where Shopify is headed. As Shopify evolves toward becoming more integrated into the transaction process, the opportunity to generate additional revenue increases. However, when you're generating revenue from transactions, there will be some impact on your gross margin due to lower gross margins associated with payment processing. I don't mind absorbing some gross margin pressure if it means Shopify captures a larger portion of the commerce stack.
  3. The third area I'd like to highlight is profitability. While I understand the desire to see dramatic margin improvement, I don't feel it's necessary to see that type of growth this quarter. What I do hope to see is continued aggressive investment spending if Shopify believes it has an attractive growth opportunity ahead of it. Additionally, I want to see that Shopify demonstrates operating discipline. If operational costs continue to grow at a slow rate relative to revenue growth and if AI tools continue to help improve internal efficiency, then perhaps investors will begin to realize that Shopify's free cash flow profile is more robust than previously thought.
  4. Finally, I'd like to highlight agentic commerce as an area that could provide the most unexpected pleasant surprises. Investors are currently divided as to whether AI commerce represents a meaningful opportunity for monetization or simply another layer of software. I think that Shopify will have an opportunity to make a greater amount of money from Agentic Commerce than most other companies do. This is because checking out, paying (for example, credit card processing), fraud prevention, collecting taxes, etc., are generally much harder to accomplish. If Shopify can provide specific numbers regarding how well their merchants are adopting this technology and what additional revenue streams may exist through agentic commerce, then there should be a very positive reaction in terms of the stock price.

Positive Reaction

Reasons for a rise in Shopify's stock price include the reporting of the following from Shopify:

  1. GMV greater than or equal to $100 billion during Q1;
  2. Q2 revenue guidance consistent with growth of approximately 30%+ per year;
  3. and, an affirmation by management that they are confident in their ability to maintain margins.

The thesis would be strengthened by concrete progress in agentic commerce adoption and monetization.

Negative Reaction

Reasons for a negative reaction in Shopify’s stock include:

  1. GMV growth below 30%
  2. poor Q2 revenue guidance;
  3. higher-than-anticipated margin compression;
  4. and/or ambiguity regarding monetization opportunities related to AI capabilities.

For now, I consider Shopify to be a "high-expectations" stock, but not overly hyped. All that needs to happen is for Shopify to demonstrate execution. If it does, I think investors may be forced to rethink their decelerating narrative.

Valuation Assessment

Shopify isn't cheap. This is simple and true.

With a price-to-forward earnings ratio (P/E) of 68x and Enterprise Value/Sales ratio of 13.8x, this stock clearly represents a premium to nearly all of the companies listed in the table. At 29.2x, Amazon's forward P/E ratio is less than half of Shopify's. Also significantly cheaper are Salesforce, at 13.5x; Adobe, at 10.2x; and MercadoLibre, with a lower Enterprise Value/Sales multiple, despite having much stronger sales growth.

Therefore, the better question is: "Is the premium worth it?"

In my opinion, the premium is worth paying, provided Shopify continues to report annualized sales growth of greater than 25-30%, along with significant improvement in free cash flow conversion. At present, Shopify is reporting 30% sales growth, while the other names mentioned here (Amazon (12.4%), Salesforce (9.6%), and Adobe (11.0%)) have reported much slower sales growth.

A company that generates such strong sales growth should deserve a premium. However, the key is that Shopify is not generating profits anywhere near those of the more mature software names. For example, Shopify's Free Cash Flow Margin is 17.4% versus 34.7% for Salesforce and 42.2% for Adobe.

This is why I do not believe you can value Shopify exactly like one values a mature software name right now. To receive a high enough valuation based upon free cash flow generated by Shopify, there must be evidence that the current rapid sales growth will eventually generate a large amount of free cash flow over time.

The most difficult comparable company for me was MercadoLibre because it is growing faster, has a higher FCF margin, and trades at a lower EV/Sales multiple. However, I do think Shopify deserves a small premium for the fact that it has greater exposure to developed markets, a clean platform model, and tremendous potential for long-term upside from AI-driven commerce.

Under the upside case, if Shopify successfully demonstrates that AI commerce can grow gross merchandise volume (GMV), increase take rates, and ultimately create new revenue opportunities, then the current multiple does not appear so extreme after all.

Risks to the Buy Thesis

The first risk is that agentic commerce takes longer to monetize than I expect. This is the most exciting part of the Shopify story, but it is also the least proven one. I can believe in AI-driven shopping, AI search, automated checkout, and new commerce flows, but investors still need evidence that this becomes real GMV and real revenue for Shopify. If management only provides general comments about AI-driven orders, merchant adoption, usage of the Universal Commerce Protocol (UCP), or future pricing, the market may begin to perceive agentic commerce as a nice narrative rather than a financial driver. At which point the multiple could contract rapidly.

My second concern is gross margin pressure from payments. Shopify is increasingly embedded in the transaction layer, and strategically speaking, I like that. However, generally speaking, payments have lower gross margins than pure software revenue. If merchant solutions growth continues to be stronger, yet the gross margin continues to decline, investors will begin to question how much operating leverage can Shopify really deliver. I would be willing to accept some gross margin compression if Shopify captures more of the commerce stack; however, there is an upper limit. If gross margin erosion accelerates rapidly, defending the valuation becomes significantly more difficult.

The third risk I have identified is "enterprise execution". This is central to my thesis as it relates to Shopify's strategy of going "up-market" with larger merchants (as they generate more Gross Merchandise Volume ("GMV"), greater payment volume, and provide for longer-term relevance on the platform). However, big brand enterprise sales cycles are generally much longer and more complicated than those for small-to-medium-sized businesses ("SMB"). Brands don't migrate overnight. Enterprise customers require integration, customization, support, and, in many cases, different pricing. Therefore, if Shopify wins logos but takes too long to monetize them into real revenue, we may see GMV growth slowing down at a rate that is faster than what has been anticipated. For me, this represents manageable risk; however, it does represent relevant risk.

Conclusion

For me, Shopify is an interesting purchase opportunity today. My rating is still "buy." In terms of multiples, the stock is not cheap, and I would not pretend otherwise. However, if the upcoming Q1 results confirm GMV to be at least $100 billion, and revenue growth of at least 30%, and they're keeping their operating expense discipline intact, my expectations for SHOP's future price target are around USD 180 within the next year.

It's essential to note that I'm basing this expectation almost entirely on how well Q1 comes out. While it doesn't have to be the "perfect" quarterly report, there has to be some kind of proof that the company still has a growing business model and that SHOP is more than an expensive software stock with a compelling storyline.

data.title: 3 Takeaways From Kevin Warsh's Fed Chair Hearings
data.description:
By Brian Levitt, Chief Global Market Strategist and Head of Strategy & Insights

This week, I’m going to take a brief break from talking about the conflict with Iran. Not because it lacks importance, but because it appears to be of fleeting interest to financial markets. Stocks hit all-time highs midweek and closed the week near those levels.1 Bond yields have generally been range-bound.2 Credit spreads have barely moved.3 The US dollar has generally weakened through April.4 In short, markets appeared to believe that the worst of the conflict is behind us and that conditions will improve over time.

At the same time, the economic data demonstrated resilience. Global manufacturing Purchasing Managers’ Index (PMI) readings, in the US and UK, remain strong and consistent with expansion.5 US retail sales surprised to the upside,6 reinforcing the view that the consumer remains engaged despite elevated gasoline prices.7 If markets are a forward-looking discounting mechanism, they appear to be telling us that geopolitics is no longer the dominant variable and that the economic backdrop remains workable.

Instead, I want to focus this week on something that I believe will matter far more to the US economy and markets over the medium to longer term. That’s the leadership transition at the Federal Reserve (Fed). The fact that Kevin Warsh is emerging as the next chairman is noteworthy on several levels. Here are my three key takeaways from his testimony to Congress last week.

1. Importance of Fed independence

There’s concern about Fed independence and the fear that Warsh may be placed at the Fed to do the president’s bidding. In his testimony, Warsh emphasized the importance of Fed independence and suggested an intention to navigate policy choices without unnecessarily provoking the administration.8

Whether that balance can be maintained over time remains an open question, but for now, investors appeared to be willing to give him the benefit of the doubt. Longer-term US inflation expectations remain well-contained,9 suggesting that markets aren’t currently pricing in concerns about political interference in monetary policy.

2. More nuanced interpretation of inflation

Warsh’s tone is increasingly dovish compared to his first go-around at the Fed as a member of the Board of Governors. Back then, he was (inexplicably?) more hawkish in the aftermath of the Global Financial Crisis. What stood out most to me during last week’s hearings was his openness to a more nuanced interpretation of inflation, especially in an environment shaped by artificial intelligence (AI)-driven productivity gains, tariffs, and oil-related shocks.

Warsh’s support of inflation measures such as median inflation, which focuses on the middle observation, and trimmed mean inflation, which excludes extreme moves on both ends, may look to some like moving the goalposts. But in the current environment, they likely make more sense, in my view. The Fed shouldn’t be setting policy based on temporary price spikes driven by tariffs, war, or supply disruptions. These alternative measures are explicitly designed to look through those shocks and better capture underlying inflation trends.

In practice, this may not represent a dramatic break from how Chair Powell approached tariffs and geopolitical risks, but Warsh appeared more willing to formalize that framework by broadening the set of inflation indicators policymakers emphasize.

3. Reducing the size of the Fed’s balance sheet - slowly

Balance sheet policy is the area where he sounded more hawkish at first, but there’s an important nuance. Warsh signaled an interest in reducing the size of the Fed’s balance sheet, while being explicit that it isn’t something that can or should happen quickly. He acknowledged that it took decades to build the balance sheet and that unwinding it will require time, patience, and care.

Just as important, any changes to balance sheet policy will require agreement across the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC), reinforcing that this would be a deliberate and collective process rather than a unilateral shift in policy.

Bottom line

The upshot is that what I heard was broadly dovish, pragmatic, and respectful of institutional independence. Markets generally seemed to agree. I view this combination as supportive of stocks, and it reinforces my longstanding inclination not to fight the Fed.

What to watch this week

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Investments cannot be made directly in an index.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

Credit spread is the difference in yield between bonds of similar maturity but with different credit quality.

Dovish refers to an economic outlook that generally supports low interest rates as a means of encouraging growth within the economy

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) is a committee of the Federal Reserve Board that meets regularly to set monetary policy, including the interest rates that are charged to banks.

Hawkish describes a central bank or policymaker's preference for a tighter monetary policy, typically to combat inflation.

Inflation is the rate at which the general price level for goods and services is increasing.

Median inflation (often measured via Median CPI) is a trimmed-mean metric representing the price change of the component in the 50th percentile of a weighted basket of goods.

Monetary easing refers to the lowering of interest rates and deposit ratios by central banks.

Option-adjusted spread (OAS) is the yield spread that must be added to a benchmark yield curve to discount a security’s payments to match its market price, using a dynamic pricing model that accounts for embedded options.

Purchasing Managers’ Indexes (PMI) are based on monthly surveys of companies worldwide and gauge business conditions within the manufacturing and services sectors.

The S&P 500® Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of the US stock market.

In general, stock values fluctuate, sometimes widely, in response to activities specific to the company as well as general market, economic, and political conditions.

A trimmed mean inflation rate is a measure of underlying inflation that calculates the average price change after removing (or trimming) a specific percentage of the most extreme, or outlier, price increases and decreases.
